result:
The clock on the wall has no numbers, only a thin black hand that keeps sweeping as if it has somewhere better to be. I like that about it. It doesn’t pretend to know exactly where we are. It only says: forward.

Outside, a bus exhales at the curb, doors folding open like a tired accordion. Someone laughs too loudly. Someone drops a bag of oranges and the oranges scatter, bright little suns rolling in every direction, and for a second the whole morning becomes a game of chance and gravity. A stranger in a suit stoops to catch one with his shoe. A child claps. The person who dropped them says thank you three times, as if gratitude works better in triplicate.

There is coffee in my cup, but not enough. There is always not enough coffee or too much thought. Steam ghosts upward and disappears into the kitchen light. The plant on the windowsill has leaned toward the glass so dramatically that it looks like it’s trying to overhear the weather. If leaves could gossip, this one would know all the neighborhood secrets: who waters at dawn, who sings while sweeping, who pretends not to cry in parked cars.

I used to believe inspiration arrived like lightning—sudden, bright, impossible to ignore. Now I think it arrives like mail: irregular, often late, occasionally misaddressed, but still a small miracle when it lands in your hands. Some days all you get is a flyer for a pizza place that closed in 2018. Other days: a handwritten envelope, your name in careful ink, and inside, one sentence that changes everything.

On the table is a bowl with a crack running through the glaze, repaired years ago with gold lacquer. I read once about that practice, how the break is not hidden but honored, made visible, made beautiful. It feels like a human thing to do. We spend so much effort disguising damage, sanding ourselves smooth for public display. But maybe the gold line is the point. Maybe surviving is an art form, and the signature is always a little jagged.

At night, the city hum lowers to a purr. Elevators sigh. Pipes tick. Somewhere, an argument blooms and fades behind thin apartment walls. Somewhere else, someone opens a book and forgets to check their phone for two entire hours, a tiny rebellion that should count as prayer. The moon hangs between buildings like a coin balanced on edge, undecided.

I think about all the versions of ourselves that never quite happened. The guitarist who quit after three lessons. The marathon runner stopped by one bad knee. The person who almost moved to Lisbon, almost learned Arabic, almost said yes, almost said no. They trail us like translucent siblings, not tragic, just unrealized. Sometimes I imagine they gather in a quiet room and trade stories about us—the one who kept going, the one who chose this life with the creaky floorboards and the overwatered basil and the inbox full of newsletters she never reads.

A pigeon lands on the fire escape and tilts its head with the solemnity of a tiny judge. Pigeons are underrated philosophers. They walk as if every step is both urgent and ridiculous, which is also true of most of human history. Empires rise, empires fall, and a pigeon still struts across the same square, unimpressed, looking for crumbs.

If I had to confess something, it would be this: I am often waiting for permission that never comes. Permission to begin before I feel ready. Permission to rest before I feel finished. Permission to take joy seriously, not as a reward but as fuel. No one sends that letter. You print it yourself, sign at the bottom, and hope your own name is enough authority.

In the drawer beside me are old receipts, a dead pen, two batteries of uncertain charge, and a photograph faded almost blue. Four people on a beach, wind whipping hair into strange shapes, all of us squinting at a sun too bright for memory to hold. One of them is gone now. Not in the dramatic way, just in the ordinary human way—moved continents, changed numbers, drifted into the vast unsearchable sea of former acquaintances. Loss is not always thunder. Sometimes it is a door closing softly in another building.

Still, there are continuities. Morning arrives with or without enthusiasm. Bread browns. Shirts wrinkle. Friends text “you up?” at exactly the wrong time. A song you forgot exists plays in a grocery store and suddenly you are seventeen again, invincible and terrified, standing under fluorescent lights pretending not to care who sees your heart.

I want to say something wise about time, but time does not need my commentary. It has excellent instincts. It turns peaches from stone to sweetness and back to rot. It teaches rivers to carve canyons through sheer insistence. It takes the sharpest grief and rounds its edges, not to erase, only to make it carryable. We call that healing because we need a gentle word for the long, stubborn labor of continuing.

The sky this evening is the color of a bruise forgetting itself. Purple loosening into pink, pink into gold, then the whole thing dimming like a theater before the first line. Across the street, a woman waters geraniums in chipped blue pots. She does it as if it matters enormously, and maybe it does. Maybe attention is the purest form of love: to notice, to tend, to return.

When I was younger, I thought meaning was hidden, buried under complexity, guarded by people with impressive credentials and excellent posture. Now I suspect meaning is embarrassingly available. It is in washing the same spoon for the thousandth time. In learning the name of the cashier who always works Tuesday nights. In saying “I don’t know” and “I’m sorry” and “stay” without negotiating your dignity first. In eating the last orange before it goes soft.

The black hand on the numberless clock keeps moving. Forward, forward. I am writing this in one long breath, not because I have solved anything, but because words are a way of keeping company with uncertainty. You set them down like stepping stones across a river you can’t fully see. Sometimes they hold. Sometimes they wobble. You cross anyway.

And if there is a conclusion, let it be small and usable: open the window. Drink the water. Answer the message. Start before you are ready. Repair what breaks with something visible. Thank people three times. Let the plant lean toward the light.
